html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Fourteen — Part Four

The Book of Melakhim Aleph

MELAKHIM ALEPH

The First Book of Kings

Chapters 19 through 22

Eliyahu at Khorev — Navot's Vineyard — Akhav's Death — The Book Closes

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

No complete DSS manuscript for Melakhim Aleph

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTERS 19 THROUGH 22 — ELIYAHU — NAVOT — AKHAV FALLS

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 19 Eliyahu Flees Izevel — Under the Juniper Tree — The Still Small Voice — Elisha Called

1 Akhav told Izevel all that Eliyahu had done, and how he had killed all the prophets with the sword.

2 Then Izevel sent a messenger to Eliyahu, saying, So may the gods do to me and more also, if I do not make your life as the life of one of them by this time tomorrow.

3 Then he was afraid, and he arose and ran for his life and came to Be'er-Sheva, which belongs to Yehudah, and left his servant there.

4 But he himself went a day's journey into the wilderness and came and sat down under a juniper tree. And he asked that he might die, saying, It is enough — now, O YHWH, take away my life, for I am no better than my fathers.

5 And he lay down and slept under a juniper tree. And behold, an angel touched him and said to him, Arise and eat.

6 And he looked, and behold, there was at his head a cake baked on hot stones and a jar of water. And he ate and drank and lay down again.

7 And the angel of YHWH came again a second time and touched him and said, Arise and eat, for the journey is too great for you.

8 And he arose and ate and drank, and went in the strength of that food forty days and forty nights to Khorev, the mount of Creator.

9 There he came to a cave and lodged in it. And behold, the word of YHWH came to him, and he said to him, What are you doing here, Eliyahu?

10 He said, I have been very jealous for YHWH, the Creator of hosts. For the people of Yisra'EL have forsaken your covenant, thrown down your altars, and killed your prophets with the sword, and I, even I only, am left, and they seek my life, to take it away.

11 And he said, Go out and stand on the mount before YHWH. And behold, YHWH passed by, and a great and strong wind tore the mountains and broke in pieces the rocks before YHWH, but YHWH was not in the wind. And after the wind an earthquake, but YHWH was not in the earthquake.

12 And after the earthquake a fire, but YHWH was not in the fire. And after the fire the sound of a low whisper.

13 And when Eliyahu heard it, he wrapped his face in his cloak and went out and stood at the entrance of the cave. And behold, there came a voice to him and said, What are you doing here, Eliyahu?

14 He said, I have been very jealous for YHWH, the Creator of hosts. For the people of Yisra'EL have forsaken your covenant, thrown down your altars, and killed your prophets with the sword, and I, even I only, am left, and they seek my life, to take it away.

15 And YHWH said to him, Go, return on your way to the wilderness of Damascus. And when you arrive, you shall anoint Khaza'el to be king over Aram.

16 And Yehu the son of Nimshi you shall anoint to be king over Yisra'EL, and Elisha the son of Shafat of Avel-Mekholah you shall anoint to be prophet in your place.

17 And the one who escapes from the sword of Khaza'el shall Yehu put to death, and the one who escapes from the sword of Yehu shall Elisha put to death.

18 Yet I will leave seven thousand in Yisra'EL, all the knees that have not bowed to Ba'al, and every mouth that has not kissed him.

19 So he departed from there and found Elisha the son of Shafat, who was plowing with twelve yoke of oxen in front of him, and he was with the twelfth. Eliyahu passed by him and cast his cloak upon him.

20 And he left the oxen and ran after Eliyahu and said, Let me kiss my father and my mother, and then I will follow you. And he said to him, Go back again, for what have I done to you?

21 And he returned from following him and took the yoke of oxen and sacrificed them and boiled their flesh with the yokes of the oxen and gave it to the people, and they ate. Then he arose and went after Eliyahu and assisted him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 19 follows the covenant prophet at his lowest point: fleeing Izevel's death threat after the greatest covenant victory of his life. The angel who feeds him under the juniper tree and the still small voice at Khorev are the covenant care of YHWH for the exhausted prophet.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 19 in full. Verse 12 — after the wind, after the earthquake, after the fire — the sound of a low whisper — is read as the covenant declaration of YHWH's mode of self-disclosure: not in the spectacular manifestations that preceded him but in the stillness that followed. The covenant presence is in the whisper.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 18 — yet I will leave seven thousand in Yisra'EL, all the knees that have not bowed to Ba'al. Eliyahu's cry — I only am left — is gently corrected: seven thousand remain. The covenant remnant is always larger than the prophet's despair allows him to see.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — it is enough — now, O YHWH, take away my life, for I am no better than my fathers. The prophet who called fire from heaven is now asking YHWH to take his life under a juniper tree. The covenant journey from Mount Karmel to the juniper tree takes one day. The despair is not a character flaw — it is the exhaustion of a human being who has sustained supernatural covenant conflict. YHWH's response is not rebuke but food and sleep — twice — verse 5-7. YHWH addresses the body before addressing the spirit. Verse 12 — the still small voice. The Hebrew qol demamah daqah — the voice of thin silence — the whisper of stillness. YHWH who was not in the great wind, not in the earthquake, not in the fire, is in the silence that follows.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 20 Ben-Hadad Besieges Samaria — Akhav's Two Victories — Akhav Spares Ben-Hadad — The Covenant Judgment

1 Ben-Hadad the king of Aram gathered all his army together. Thirty-two kings were with him, and horses and chariots. And he went up and besieged Samaria and fought against it.

2 And he sent messengers into the city to Akhav king of Yisra'EL and said to him, Thus says Ben-Hadad:

3 Your silver and your gold are mine, your wives also and your children — the best of them are mine.

4 And the king of Yisra'EL answered, As you say, my lord, O king, I am yours, and all that I have.

5 The messengers came again and said, Thus says Ben-Hadad: I sent to you, saying, Deliver to me your silver and your gold, your wives and your children.

6 Nevertheless I will send my servants to you tomorrow about this time, and they shall search your house and the houses of your servants and lay hands on whatever pleases them and take it away.

7 Then the king of Yisra'EL called all the elders of the land and said, Mark, now, and see how this man is seeking trouble, for he sent to me for my wives and my children, and for my silver and my gold, and I did not refuse him.

8 And all the elders and all the people said to him, Do not listen or consent.

9 So he said to the messengers of Ben-Hadad, Tell my lord the king, All that you first demanded of your servant I will do, but this thing I cannot do. And the messengers departed and brought him word again.

10 Ben-Hadad sent to him and said, The gods do so to me and more also, if the dust of Samaria shall suffice for handfuls for all the people who follow me.

11 And the king of Yisra'EL answered, Tell him, Let not him who straps on his armor boast himself as he who takes it off.

12 When Ben-Hadad heard this message, as he was drinking with the kings in the booths, he said to his servants, Take your positions. And they took their positions against the city.

13 And behold, a prophet came near to Akhav king of Yisra'EL and said, Thus says YHWH, Have you seen all this great multitude? Behold, I will give it into your hand this day, and you shall know that I am YHWH.

14 And Akhav said, By whom? He said, Thus says YHWH, By the servants of the governors of the districts. Then he said, Who shall begin the battle? He answered, You.

15 Then he mustered the servants of the governors of the districts, and they were two hundred and thirty-two. And after them he mustered all the people of Yisra'EL, seven thousand.

16 And they went out at noon, while Ben-Hadad was drinking himself drunk in the booths, he and the thirty-two kings who helped him.

17 The servants of the governors of the districts went out first. And Ben-Hadad sent out scouts, and they reported to him, Men are coming out from Samaria.

18 He said, If they have come out for peace, take them alive. Or if they have come out for war, take them alive.

19 So these went out of the city, the servants of the governors of the districts and the army that followed them.

20 And each struck down his man. The Arameans fled, and Yisra'EL pursued them, but Ben-Hadad king of Aram escaped on a horse with horsemen.

21 And the king of Yisra'EL went out and struck the horses and chariots, and struck the Arameans with a great blow.

22 Then the prophet came near to the king of Yisra'EL and said to him, Come, strengthen yourself, and consider well what you have to do, for in the spring the king of Aram will come up against you.

23 And the servants of the king of Aram said to him, Their gods are gods of the hills, and so they were stronger than we. But let us fight against them in the plain, and surely we shall be stronger than they.

24 And do this: remove the kings, each from his post, and put commanders in their places,

25 And muster an army like the army that you have lost, horse for horse, and chariot for chariot. Then we will fight against them in the plain, and surely we shall be stronger than they. And he listened to their voice and did so.

26 In the spring, Ben-Hadad mustered the Arameans and went up to Afek to fight against Yisra'EL.

27 And the people of Yisra'EL were mustered and were provisioned and went against them. The people of Yisra'EL encamped before them like two little flocks of goats, but the Arameans filled the country.

28 And a man of Creator came near and said to the king of Yisra'EL, Thus says YHWH, Because the Arameans have said, YHWH is a god of the hills but he is not a god of the valleys, therefore I will give all this great multitude into your hand, and you shall know that I am YHWH.

29 And they encamped opposite one another seven days. Then on the seventh day the battle was joined. And the people of Yisra'EL struck down of the Arameans a hundred thousand foot soldiers in one day.

30 And the rest fled into the city of Afek, and the wall fell upon twenty-seven thousand men who were left. Ben-Hadad also fled and entered an inner chamber in the city.

31 And his servants said to him, Behold now, we have heard that the kings of the house of Yisra'EL are merciful kings. Let us put sackcloth around our waists and ropes on our heads and go out to the king of Yisra'EL. Perhaps he will spare your life.

32 So they tied sackcloth around their waists and put ropes on their heads and went to the king of Yisra'EL and said, Your servant Ben-Hadad says, Please let me live. And he said, Does he still live? He is my brother.

33 Now the men were watching for a sign, and they quickly took it up from him and said, Yes, Ben-Hadad is your brother. Then he said, Go and bring him. Then Ben-Hadad came out to him, and he caused him to come up into the chariot.

34 And Ben-Hadad said to him, The cities that my father took from your father I will restore, and you may establish bazaars for yourself in Damascus, as my father did in Samaria. And Akhav said, I will let you go on these terms. So he made a covenant with him and let him go.

35 And a certain man of the sons of the prophets said to his fellow at the command of YHWH, Strike me, please. But the man refused to strike him.

36 Then he said to him, Because you have not obeyed the voice of YHWH, behold, as soon as you have gone from me, a lion shall strike you down. And as he went from him, a lion met him and struck him down.

37 Then he found another man and said, Strike me, please. And the man struck him and wounded him.

38 So the prophet departed and waited for the king by the way, disguising himself with a bandage over his eyes.

39 And as the king passed, he cried to the king and said, Your servant went out into the midst of the battle, and behold, a soldier turned and brought a man to me and said, Guard this man. If by any means he is missing, your life shall be for his life, or else you shall pay a talent of silver.

40 And as your servant was busy here and there, he was gone. The king of Yisra'EL said to him, So shall your judgment be — you yourself have decided it.

41 Then he hurried to take the bandage away from his eyes, and the king of Yisra'EL recognized him as one of the prophets.

42 And he said to him, Thus says YHWH, Because you have let go out of your hand the man whom I had devoted to destruction, therefore your life shall be for his life, and your people for his people.

43 And the king of Yisra'EL went to his house vexed and sullen and came to Samaria.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 20 contains the covenant paradox of Akhav's career: YHWH gives him two military victories over Ben-Hadad — one on the hills and one on the plains — specifically to refute the Aramean theology that YHWH is only a god of the hills. Then Akhav violates the covenant by sparing Ben-Hadad whom YHWH had devoted to destruction.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 20 in full. Verse 28 — because the Arameans have said YHWH is a god of the hills but not a god of the valleys, therefore I will give all this great multitude into your hand — is read as the covenant declaration of YHWH's universal sovereignty. The theological claim of the enemy determines the shape of the covenant response.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 42 — because you have let go out of your hand the man whom I had devoted to destruction, therefore your life shall be for his life, and your people for his people. The covenant principle of herem — devotion to destruction — violated produces the same consequence as in Yehoshua 7: the life of the violator replaces the life of the one who should have been destroyed.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 32-34 — Ben-Hadad came out to Akhav and was caused to come up into the chariot — and Akhav said, I will let you go on these terms and made a covenant with him. The Arameans put sackcloth and ropes on their heads in the posture of defeated submission. Akhav calls Ben-Hadad my brother and releases him for a trade agreement. The commercial logic overrides the covenant requirement. YHWH's response — verse 42 — your life shall be for his life — is the covenant accounting: what was devoted to destruction will be paid for in kind.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 21 Navot's Vineyard — Izevel's Plot — Navot Stoned — Eliyahu's Condemnation — YHWH Pronounces Judgment

1 Now Navot the Yizreelite had a vineyard in Yizre'el, beside the palace of Akhav king of Samaria.

2 And after this Akhav said to Navot, Give me your vineyard, that I may have it for a vegetable garden, because it is near my house, and I will give you a better vineyard for it, or, if it seems good to you, I will give you its value in money.

3 But Navot said to Akhav, YHWH forbid that I should give you the inheritance of my fathers.

4 And Akhav went into his house vexed and sullen because of what Navot the Yizreelite had said to him, for he had said, I will not give you the inheritance of my fathers. And he lay down on his bed and turned away his face and would eat no food.

5 But Izevel his wife came to him and said to him, Why is your spirit so vexed that you eat no food?

6 And he said to her, Because I spoke to Navot the Yizreelite and said to him, Give me your vineyard for money, or else, if it please you, I will give you another vineyard for it. And he answered, I will not give you my vineyard.

7 And Izevel his wife said to him, Do you now govern Yisra'EL? Arise and eat bread and let your heart be cheerful. I will give you the vineyard of Navot the Yizreelite.

8 So she wrote letters in Akhav's name and sealed them with his seal, and she sent the letters to the elders and the leaders who lived with Navot in his city.

9 And she wrote in the letters, Proclaim a fast, and set Navot at the head of the people.

10 And set two worthless men opposite him, and let them bring a charge against him, saying, You have cursed Creator and the king. Then take him out and stone him to death.

11 And the men of his city, the elders and the leaders who lived in his city, did as Izevel had sent word to them. As it was written in the letters that she had sent to them,

12 They proclaimed a fast and set Navot at the head of the people.

13 And the two worthless men came in and sat opposite him. And the worthless men brought a charge against Navot in the presence of the people, saying, Navot cursed Creator and the king. So they took him outside the city and stoned him to death with stones.

14 Then they sent to Izevel, saying, Navot has been stoned — he is dead.

15 As soon as Izevel heard that Navot had been stoned and was dead, Izevel said to Akhav, Arise, take possession of the vineyard of Navot the Yizreelite, which he refused to give you for money, for Navot is not alive but dead.

16 And as soon as Akhav heard that Navot was dead, Akhav arose to go down to the vineyard of Navot the Yizreelite, to take possession of it.

17 Then the word of YHWH came to Eliyahu the Tishbite, saying,

18 Arise, go down to meet Akhav king of Yisra'EL, who is in Samaria. Behold, he is in the vineyard of Navot, where he has gone to take possession.

19 And you shall say to him, Thus says YHWH: Have you killed and also taken possession? And you shall say to him, Thus says YHWH: In the place where dogs licked up the blood of Navot shall dogs lick your own blood.

20 Akhav said to Eliyahu, Have you found me, O my enemy? He said, I have found you, because you have sold yourself to do what is evil in the sight of YHWH.

21 Behold, I will bring disaster upon you. I will utterly burn you up, and will cut off from Akhav every male, bond or free, in Yisra'EL.

22 And I will make your house like the house of Yarovam the son of Nevat, and like the house of Baasha the son of Akhiyah, for the anger to which you have provoked me, and because you have made Yisra'EL to sin.

23 And of Izevel YHWH also said, The dogs shall eat Izevel within the walls of Yizre'el.

24 Anyone belonging to Akhav who dies in the city the dogs shall eat, and anyone of his who dies in the open country the birds of the heavens shall eat.

25 There was none who sold himself to do what was evil in the sight of YHWH like Akhav, whom Izevel his wife incited.

26 He acted very abominably in going after idols, as the Amorites had done, whom YHWH cast out before the people of Yisra'EL.

27 And when Akhav heard those words, he tore his clothes and put sackcloth on his flesh and fasted and lay in sackcloth and went about dejectedly.

28 And the word of YHWH came to Eliyahu the Tishbite, saying,

29 Have you seen how Akhav has humbled himself before me? Because he has humbled himself before me, I will not bring the disaster in his days, but in his son's days I will bring the disaster upon his house.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. The Navot narrative is the covenant law case at the heart of the Akhav-Eliyahu conflict. Navot's refusal — YHWH forbid that I should give you the inheritance of my fathers — is the covenant law of Bamidbar 36 and Vayikra 25 applied: the ancestral land inheritance cannot be sold permanently.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 21 in full. Verse 29 — because Akhav has humbled himself before me, I will not bring the disaster in his days. Even the covenant judgment against Akhav is modified by his genuine response of humility. The covenant principle: YHWH responds to the posture of the heart even when the covenant violation was severe.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 19 — in the place where dogs licked up the blood of Navot shall dogs lick your own blood — the covenant precision of the judgment mirrors the crime exactly. The location, the blood, the dogs. Eliyahu's prophecy is fulfilled in Chapter 22:38 and the dogs lick Izevel's blood in Melakhim Bet 9:36.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — Navot said, YHWH forbid that I should give you the inheritance of my fathers. The single most important verse in the chapter. Navot does not negotiate, does not offer a counter-proposal, does not appeal to the king's power. He invokes YHWH's name as the ground of his refusal. The covenant land inheritance — given by YHWH to the fathers — cannot be surrendered to the king. Akhav's response — verse 4 — he went to bed vexed and turned his face to the wall. The king of Yisra'EL pouts like a child because a vineyard owner said no. Izevel's response — verse 7 — do you now govern Yisra'EL? — reveals the fundamental difference between Zidonian and Israelite political theology: in Izevel's world, a king simply takes what he wants. In covenant Yisra'EL, a king cannot.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 22 Mikhayehu's True Prophecy — Akhav Disguises Himself — Struck by a Random Arrow — The Dogs Lick His Blood

1 For three years Aram and Yisra'EL continued without war.

2 But in the third year Yehoshafat king of Yehudah came down to the king of Yisra'EL.

3 And the king of Yisra'EL said to his servants, Do you know that Ramot-Gil'ad belongs to us, and we keep quiet and do not take it out of the hand of the king of Aram?

4 And he said to Yehoshafat, Will you go with me to battle at Ramot-Gil'ad? And Yehoshafat said to the king of Yisra'EL, I am as you are, my people as your people, my horses as your horses.

5 And Yehoshafat said to the king of Yisra'EL, Inquire first for the word of YHWH.

6 Then the king of Yisra'EL gathered the prophets together, about four hundred men, and said to them, Shall I go to battle against Ramot-Gil'ad, or shall I refrain? And they said, Go up, for the Lord will give it into the hand of the king.

7 But Yehoshafat said, Is there not here another prophet of YHWH of whom we may inquire?

8 And the king of Yisra'EL said to Yehoshafat, There is yet one man by whom we may inquire of YHWH, Mikhayehu the son of Yimlah, but I hate him, for he never prophesies good concerning me, but evil. And Yehoshafat said, Let not the king say so.

9 Then the king of Yisra'EL summoned an officer and said, Bring quickly Mikhayehu the son of Yimlah.

10 Now the king of Yisra'EL and Yehoshafat the king of Yehudah were sitting on their thrones, arrayed in their robes, at the threshing floor at the entrance of the gate of Samaria, and all the prophets were prophesying before them.

11 And Tzidkiyahu the son of Kena'anah made for himself horns of iron and said, Thus says YHWH, With these you shall push the Arameans until they are destroyed.

12 And all the prophets prophesied so and said, Go up to Ramot-Gil'ad and triumph — YHWH will give it into the hand of the king.

13 And the messenger who went to summon Mikhayehu said to him, Behold, the words of the prophets with one accord are favorable to the king. Let your word be like the word of one of them, and speak favorably.

14 But Mikhayehu said, As YHWH lives, what YHWH says to me, that I will speak.

15 And when he had come to the king, the king said to him, Mikhayehu, shall we go to Ramot-Gil'ad to battle, or shall we refrain? And he answered him, Go up and triumph — YHWH will give it into the hand of the king.

16 But the king said to him, How many times shall I make you swear that you speak to me nothing but the truth in the name of YHWH?

17 And he said, I saw all Yisra'EL scattered on the mountains, as sheep that have no shepherd. And YHWH said, These have no master — let each return to his home in peace.

18 And the king of Yisra'EL said to Yehoshafat, Did I not tell you that he would not prophesy good concerning me, but evil?

19 And Mikhayehu said, Therefore hear the word of YHWH: I saw YHWH sitting on his throne, and all the host of heaven standing beside him on his right hand and on his left.

20 And YHWH said, Who will entice Akhav, that he may go up and fall at Ramot-Gil'ad? And one said one thing, and another said another.

21 Then a spirit came forward and stood before YHWH, saying, I will entice him.

22 And YHWH said to him, By what means? And he said, I will go out, and will be a lying spirit in the mouth of all his prophets. And he said, You are to entice him, and you shall succeed — go out and do so.

23 Now therefore behold, YHWH has put a lying spirit in the mouth of all these your prophets. YHWH has declared disaster for you.

24 Then Tzidkiyahu the son of Kena'anah came near and struck Mikhayehu on the cheek and said, How did the Spirit of YHWH go from me to speak to you?

25 And Mikhayehu said, Behold, you shall see on that day when you go into an inner chamber to hide yourself.

26 And the king of Yisra'EL said, Seize Mikhayehu, and take him back to Amon the governor of the city and to Yo'ash the king's son,

27 And say, Thus says the king, Put this fellow in prison and feed him with meager rations of bread and water, until I come in peace.

28 And Mikhayehu said, If you return in peace, YHWH has not spoken by me. And he said, Hear, all you peoples.

29 So the king of Yisra'EL and Yehoshafat the king of Yehudah went up to Ramot-Gil'ad.

30 And the king of Yisra'EL said to Yehoshafat, I will disguise myself and go into battle, but you wear your robes. And the king of Yisra'EL disguised himself and went into battle.

31 Now the king of Aram had commanded the thirty-two captains of his chariots, Fight with neither small nor great, but only with the king of Yisra'EL.

32 And when the captains of the chariots saw Yehoshafat, they said, It is surely the king of Yisra'EL. So they turned to fight against him. And Yehoshafat cried out.

33 And when the captains of the chariots saw that it was not the king of Yisra'EL, they turned back from pursuing him.

34 But a certain man drew his bow at random and struck the king of Yisra'EL between the scale armor and the breastplate. Therefore he said to the driver of his chariot, Turn around and carry me out of the battle, for I am wounded.

35 And the battle continued that day, and the king was propped up in his chariot facing the Arameans, until at evening he died. And the blood of his wound flowed into the bottom of the chariot.

36 And about sunset a cry went through the army, Every man to his city, and every man to his country.

37 So the king died, and was brought to Samaria. And they buried the king in Samaria.

38 And they washed the chariot by the pool of Samaria, and the dogs licked up his blood, and the prostitutes washed themselves in it, according to the word of YHWH that he had spoken.

39 Now the rest of the acts of Akhav and all that he did, and the ivory house that he built and all the cities that he built, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Yisra'EL?

40 So Akhav slept with his fathers, and Akhazyahu his son reigned in his place.

41 Yehoshafat the son of Asa began to reign over Yehudah in the fourth year of Akhav king of Yisra'EL.

42 Yehoshafat was thirty-five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned twenty-five years in Yerushalayim. His mother's name was Azuvah the daughter of Shilkhi.

43 And he walked in all the way of Asa his father. He did not turn aside from it, doing what was right in the sight of YHWH. Yet the high places were not taken away, and the people still sacrificed and made offerings on the high places.

44 Yehoshafat also made peace with the king of Yisra'EL.

45 Now the rest of the acts of Yehoshafat, and his might that he showed, and how he warred, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Yehudah?

46 And the remnant of the male cult prostitutes who remained in the days of his father Asa, he exterminated from the land.

47 There was no king in Edom — a deputy was king.

48 Yehoshafat made ships of Tarshish to go to Ofir for gold, but they did not go, for the ships were wrecked at Ezyon-Gever.

49 Then Akhazyahu the son of Akhav said to Yehoshafat, Let my servants go with your servants in the ships. But Yehoshafat was not willing.

50 And Yehoshafat slept with his fathers and was buried with his fathers in the city of Dawid his father, and Yehoram his son reigned in his place.

51 Akhazyahu the son of Akhav became king over Yisra'EL in Samaria in the seventeenth year of Yehoshafat king of Yehudah, and he reigned two years over Yisra'EL.

52 He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH and walked in the way of his father and in the way of his mother and in the way of Yarovam the son of Nevat, who made Yisra'EL to sin.

53 He served Ba'al and worshiped him and provoked YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, to anger, according to all that his father had done.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 22 closes Melakhim Aleph with the death of Akhav — struck by a random arrow between the scale armor and the breastplate while disguised. No human plan arranged this. YHWH's covenant judgment against Akhav was executed by an arrow shot at random that found the one gap in the disguised king's armor.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 22 in full. Verse 14 — Mikhayehu said, as YHWH lives, what YHWH says to me, that I will speak — is read as the covenant declaration of the true prophet: the word of YHWH takes precedence over royal pressure, favorable consensus, and personal safety.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 38 — the dogs licked up his blood — according to the word of YHWH that he had spoken. The covenant prophecy of Eliyahu in Chapter 21:19 — in the place where dogs licked up the blood of Navot shall dogs lick your own blood — is fulfilled. The covenant word always reaches its mark.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 34 — a certain man drew his bow at random and struck the king between the scale armor and the breastplate. The Hebrew letummo — at his completeness, at random, innocently — describes a shot without aim. No human intent was required. Akhav had disguised himself specifically to avoid being targeted — verse 30. The Aramean commanders were specifically ordered to target only the king of Yisra'EL — verse 31. The disguise defeated the human plan. It did not defeat YHWH's covenant word. A random arrow finds the one gap in the armor of the disguised king. Mikhayehu's prophecy — verse 17 — Yisra'EL scattered on the mountains like sheep without a shepherd — is fulfilled by an innocent arrow. The covenant word does not require human cooperation to accomplish what it declares.

END OF PART FOUR — CHAPTERS 19 THROUGH 22 — MELAKHIM ALEPH COMPLETE
